THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Forty-Five -- Part Two

The Temple Scroll

11Q19 -- THE TEMPLE SCROLL

Creator Speaks Directly to Mosheh -- 66 Columns -- Cave 11 Qumran

Columns 23 through 44 -- Temple Architecture and Purity Laws

The Slaughter House -- The Stairways -- The Three Courts -- The Towers -- The Sabbath Laws -- The Vow and the Nazirite -- The Purity of the City -- The Burial of the Dead -- The Judges -- The King and His Council -- The Covenant Blessings and Curses

Dead Sea Scrolls -- Cave 11 Qumran -- 11Q19 -- Hebrew Primary

The Longest Single DSS Manuscript -- 66 Columns -- Approx 8.6 Meters

CONCERN LEVEL: INTERNAL COMPLEXITY ONLY -- Creator Speaks in First Person Throughout

YHWH and The Creator only

THE TEMPLE SCROLL -- 11Q19 -- COLUMNS 23 THROUGH 44

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 23 The Pavement of the Inner Court — Its Stones — The Drains — The Slaughter House — Where the Burnt Offerings Are Slaughtered — The Blood and Its Disposal

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. No external tradition has claimed this text or used it to support false deity theology. The only concern noted is entirely internal to covenant scholarship: the Temple Scroll presents itself as direct divine speech -- Creator addressing Mosheh in the first person throughout all 66 columns. This is a strong claim of canonical authority that the Qumran community appears to have accepted at near-Torah level. The covenant content itself -- expanded festival laws, temple architecture, purity regulations, covenant statutes -- is entirely consistent with and grounded in the Mosaic Torah tradition. There is nothing in this text that a covenant-faithful reader should be alarmed by. Read this text as what it is: the most extensive surviving covenant legal document from Qumran -- pure Torah-rooted covenant legislation with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 And you shall pave the inner court with fine stone — alabaster and marble and polished stone — and the joints shall be sealed with lead so no blood seeps beneath.

2 And you shall make drains in the pavement of the inner court — the blood of the burnt offerings and the sin offerings shall flow through the drains to the outside of the city.

3 And you shall make a slaughter house in the northwest corner of the inner court — where the burnt offerings are slaughtered and their blood collected before the altar.

4 And the slaughter house shall have hooks in its walls and posts set in the floor — for hanging the animals while they are skinned and prepared.

5 And no blood of the offerings shall be left standing in the court — all blood shall be drained immediately and carried away through the channels I have appointed.

6 And the fat portions of the offerings shall be carried immediately to the altar and burned — they shall not be left overnight in the court.

7 And the priests shall carry the ashes of the altar outside the city to the place I have appointed for them — the place of ashes shall be to the east of the city.

8 And no unclean person shall touch the offerings or carry them or handle them in any way — only the clean priests shall handle the holy things.

9 The blood of the Pesach offering shall be poured at the base of the altar — it shall not be eaten with leaven and it shall not remain until morning.

10 Whatever remains of the offering until morning you shall burn with fire — you shall not break any of its bones.

11 And the portions of the offerings assigned to the priests — the breast and the right thigh — shall be eaten by the priests in the inner court on the day of the offering.

12 Nothing of the most holy offerings shall be carried outside the inner court — they shall be eaten before YHWH in the place I have sanctified for my name.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 23 covers the practical arrangements for slaughter and blood disposal in the inner court. The detailed drainage system — sealed pavement with channels carrying blood outside the city — reflects the Temple Scroll's concern that blood not contaminate the sanctuary precincts. The slaughterhouse placement in the northwest corner mirrors the archaeological evidence of the Solomonic and Second Temple period installations. The prohibition on leaving offerings until morning (verse 10) follows the Pesach legislation of Column 3 and Shemot 12:10.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Vayikra 1-7 — the complete offering legislation including the disposal of blood, fat, and ashes — which underlies Column 23. The Orit Ge'ez Vayikra 4:7 specifies that the blood of the sin offering is poured at the base of the altar — preserved exactly in verse 9 of Column 23. The Orit Ge'ez Vayikra 6:8-13 covers the ashes of the burnt offering and their disposal outside the camp — the Temple Scroll applies this to the sanctuary city in verse 7.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Vayikra 1-7 and Shemot 12:10 provide the base texts for Column 23's offering disposal legislation. The Masoretic Vayikra 7:30-36 specifies the priestly portions — breast and right thigh — that verse 11 of Column 23 requires the priests to eat in the inner court. The Temple Scroll's requirement that most holy offerings be eaten within the inner court (verse 12) is more stringent than the Masoretic requirement of eating within the court of the tabernacle generally.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — the blood shall flow through the drains to the outside of the city. The Temple Scroll's blood disposal system — sealed pavement, drains, channels carrying blood outside — reflects the covenant community's understanding that blood, even the holy blood of covenant offerings, is a source of impurity once it has served its altar function. The same blood that effects atonement when sprinkled at the altar becomes a purity threat when it pools in the court. The drainage system is the covenant architectural solution to this theological paradox.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 24 The Stairways of the Middle Court — The Colonnades — The Chambers of the Middle Court — Their Uses — The Storage of the Tithes and Firstfruits — The Levitical Assignments

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. No external tradition has claimed this text or used it to support false deity theology. The only concern noted is entirely internal to covenant scholarship: the Temple Scroll presents itself as direct divine speech -- Creator addressing Mosheh in the first person throughout all 66 columns. This is a strong claim of canonical authority that the Qumran community appears to have accepted at near-Torah level. The covenant content itself -- expanded festival laws, temple architecture, purity regulations, covenant statutes -- is entirely consistent with and grounded in the Mosaic Torah tradition. There is nothing in this text that a covenant-faithful reader should be alarmed by. Read this text as what it is: the most extensive surviving covenant legal document from Qumran -- pure Torah-rooted covenant legislation with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 And you shall make stairways going up from the outer court to the middle court — each stairway shall be fourteen steps wide and its rise shall be a half cubit per step.

2 And between the middle court and the inner court there shall be stairways of twelve steps — the same width and rise — ascending to the gates of the inner court.

3 And along the inner wall of the middle court you shall make colonnades — their columns shall be of cedar and their capitals of gold and their bases of silver.

4 And between the columns there shall be chambers for the Levites who serve in the middle court — each chamber shall be designated for a specific Levitical function.

5 The chambers of the south colonnade shall be for the storage of the firstfruits of grain — the firstfruits of wheat and barley and spelt.

6 The chambers of the north colonnade shall be for the firstfruits of wine and oil and honey — they shall be kept there until the priests take their portions.

7 The chambers of the east colonnade shall be for the tithes of the flock and the herd — one of every ten shall be brought there and kept until the feast.

8 The chambers of the west colonnade shall be for the firstfruits of wood — the wood offerings of the tribes shall be stored there according to their appointed times.

9 And the Levites who serve at the gate of the middle court shall have their chambers at the gate — they shall not leave their post on the days of the feast.

10 And the portions of the Levites — a tenth of the tithe — shall be given to them from the storage chambers according to their service.

11 No man shall take anything from the storage chambers without the authority of the priest in charge — everything shall be accounted for and distributed according to the covenant order.

12 For the tithes and firstfruits are holy to YHWH — they are the covenant acknowledgment that all the produce of the land is his gift and he requires the first of every gift before the rest may be used.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 24 details the stairway system connecting the three courts and the colonnade chambers of the middle court, with specific storage assignments for each colonnade direction. The four-direction assignment — grain south, wine and oil north, livestock tithes east, wood west — creates a systematic mapping of all covenant agricultural produce to specific physical locations in the sanctuary complex. No produce category is without its designated space; every covenant offering has its appointed place.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Bamidbar 18:21-32 — the Levitical tithe legislation — and Devarim 14:22-29 — the tithe and firstfruit regulations — which underlie Column 24's storage chamber system. The Orit Ge'ez also preserves Nekhemyah 10:37-39 — the actual implementation of the firstfruit and tithe storage rooms in the Second Temple period — confirming that the Temple Scroll's storage system corresponds to historical covenant practice.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Bamidbar 18 and Devarim 14 provide the covenant authority for the tithe and firstfruit storage system of Column 24. The Masoretic Nekhemyah 13:5 — the large chamber where the grain offering, frankincense, vessels, and tithes of grain, wine, and oil were stored — confirms the historical existence of the kind of storage system the Temple Scroll legislates. The Temple Scroll is not innovating but claiming divine authority for the covenant storage practice documented in the historical books.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — the tithes and firstfruits are holy to YHWH — they are the covenant acknowledgment that all the produce of the land is his gift. The Temple Scroll's theology of tithes and firstfruits is not primarily financial but covenantal: the tithe is the formal acknowledgment that Creator owns the land and its produce. The covenant people are tenants who owe a portion to the owner. Withholding the tithe is not merely economic default — it is covenant rebellion, a denial of Creator's ownership of the covenant land.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 25 The Outer Court — Its Dimensions — Its Twelve Gates Named for the Tribes — The Colonnades of the Outer Court — The Accommodation of the People — The Sacred Meals

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. No external tradition has claimed this text or used it to support false deity theology. The only concern noted is entirely internal to covenant scholarship: the Temple Scroll presents itself as direct divine speech -- Creator addressing Mosheh in the first person throughout all 66 columns. This is a strong claim of canonical authority that the Qumran community appears to have accepted at near-Torah level. The covenant content itself -- expanded festival laws, temple architecture, purity regulations, covenant statutes -- is entirely consistent with and grounded in the Mosaic Torah tradition. There is nothing in this text that a covenant-faithful reader should be alarmed by. Read this text as what it is: the most extensive surviving covenant legal document from Qumran -- pure Torah-rooted covenant legislation with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 And you shall make the outer court outside the middle court — its length from east to west shall be one thousand six hundred cubits and its width from north to south shall be one thousand six hundred cubits.

2 And the outer court shall be enclosed by a wall of great stones — its height shall be thirty cubits and at its base it shall be nine cubits wide.

3 And you shall make twelve gates in the outer court — three on each side — each gate shall be fifty cubits wide and seventy cubits high.

4 And you shall name the gates for the twelve tribes of Yisra'EL — three tribes on each side — according to the order of the encampment in the wilderness.

5 And along the inner wall of the outer court you shall make colonnades — their columns shall be of cedar overlaid with gold and their bases of bronze.

6 And between the columns there shall be chambers — the chambers of the outer colonnade shall be for the people of Yisra'EL — for those who come up to the feasts.

7 Each man and his household shall have a chamber in which they may eat their tithe meals and their peace offering meals before YHWH their Creator.

8 No unclean person shall eat in these chambers — only those who are clean from every uncleanness shall eat before me in the place I have appointed.

9 And the chambers shall be open to all the twelve tribes — no tribe shall be turned away from the place of the covenant meals.

10 And you shall make provision for the widows and orphans and strangers who have no chamber of their own — the Levites shall provide for them from the tithe chambers.

11 For my covenant requires that all who come to my house shall eat before me — the poor shall not be excluded from the covenant meal.

12 I am YHWH your Creator who brought you out of Egypt — you shall remember that you were slaves and YHWH your Creator redeemed you — therefore you shall include all who come to my house in the covenant feast.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 25 provides the outer court dimensions and the chamber system for the covenant meals of the entire people of Yisra'EL. The one thousand six hundred cubit outer court creates the largest of the three concentric squares — an immense sacred precinct capable of accommodating the entire covenant people for the three pilgrimage feasts. The chamber system for tithe meals and peace offering meals in verse 7 reflects the covenant requirement of Devarim 12 that the covenant people eat their tithes before YHWH in the chosen place — the Temple Scroll provides the physical infrastructure for this covenant requirement.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 12:5-19 — the requirement to eat the tithes and peace offerings before YHWH in the chosen place — which provides the covenant authority for Column 25's chamber system. The Orit Ge'ez also preserves Devarim 16:11-14 — the requirement to rejoice before YHWH at the feast including the Levite, the sojourner, the orphan, and the widow — which is reflected in verses 10-11 of Column 25. The Temple Scroll provides the physical infrastructure; the Orit Ge'ez Devarim tradition provides the theological mandate.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 12:5-19 and 16:11-14 provide the foundational covenant texts for Column 25. The Masoretic requirement to eat the tithe in the chosen place — you shall eat before YHWH your Creator and you shall rejoice — is here given its full architectural realisation: twelve hundred chambers in the outer court colonnade accommodating every family of every tribe. The Temple Scroll transforms the Masoretic covenant requirement into a precise architectural provision.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11 — the poor shall not be excluded from the covenant meal. The Temple Scroll's explicit provision for the poor, widows, orphans, and strangers in the covenant feast space is the most socially inclusive statement in the entire scroll. The enormous outer court with its twelve hundred chambers is not merely accommodation for the wealthy pilgrims — it is specifically designed to include the most economically marginal members of the covenant community. The covenant feast is a covenant right of all Yisra'EL, not a privilege of those with resources.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 26 The Tower of the Outer Court — Its Height and Construction — The Lookout — The Wall Patrols — The Priests Who Guard the Outer Wall — No Uncircumcised Shall Enter

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. No external tradition has claimed this text or used it to support false deity theology. The only concern noted is entirely internal to covenant scholarship: the Temple Scroll presents itself as direct divine speech -- Creator addressing Mosheh in the first person throughout all 66 columns. 1 And you shall make towers at the corners of the outer court — each tower shall be one hundred cubits high and its base shall be twenty cubits square.

2 And on top of each tower there shall be a lookout platform — from which the priests who guard the sanctuary can see in all directions.

3 And you shall appoint priests to patrol the outer wall — going around continuously both day and night — that no unclean person may approach the sanctuary precincts.

4 The priests who guard the outer wall shall be armed — not with weapons of war but with the authority of the covenant — and they shall turn away all who are unclean.

5 No uncircumcised male shall enter the outer court at any time — the covenant sign of circumcision is the condition of entry for all males.

6 And no Gentile who has not joined himself to the covenant of Yisra'EL shall enter the outer court — the sanctuary is for the covenant people.

7 But any man who has joined himself to the covenant — who has been circumcised and immersed and has accepted the statutes of Yisra'EL — shall be as a native-born.

8 And you shall make inscriptions at every gate of the outer court — stating the conditions of entry — so that all who come may know what is required.

9 And the priests who serve at the gate shall examine all who enter — they shall ask each man concerning his cleanness before allowing him to pass through the gate.

10 Any man who enters the outer court knowing himself to be unclean has profaned the sanctuary — he shall be brought before the judges and shall bear his iniquity.

11 For I dwell in the midst of the children of Yisra'EL — my sanctuary is in their midst — and they shall not defile it with their uncleanness.

12 Keep my sanctuary holy — for I YHWH who sanctify Yisra'EL am holy — and I require that all who approach me be holy as I am holy.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 26 covers the outer court towers and the guard system for the sanctuary perimeter. The corner towers one hundred cubits high create the visual dominance of the sanctuary city over the surrounding landscape — a physical expression of the theological claim that the sanctuary is the centre of the covenant world. The examination of entrants at the gate (verse 9) institutionalises the same concern as the Damascus Document's membership examination process: entry to the covenant community requires verification of covenant status.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Yehezkel 44:5-9 — the prohibition on uncircumcised foreigners entering the sanctuary — which provides the covenant authority for verses 5-6 of Column 26. The Orit Ge'ez also preserves Shemot 12:48 — no uncircumcised person shall eat of the Pesach — which grounds the circumcision requirement in the foundational covenant act of the Exodus. The Temple Scroll extends this Exodus requirement to the entire sanctuary precincts.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Yehezkel 44:5-9 provides the prophetic authority for the uncircumcised exclusion. The Masoretic Devarim 23:2-9 provides the covenant legislation on who may enter the assembly of YHWH. The Temple Scroll integrates both the prophetic and the Mosaic legislative traditions into a comprehensive entry requirement for the outer court. The examination system of verse 9 is the practical implementation of what the Mosaic tradition legislates in principle.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7 — any man who has joined himself to the covenant — who has been circumcised and immersed and has accepted the statutes of Yisra'EL — shall be as a native-born. The Temple Scroll's three-element conversion requirement — circumcision, immersion, and acceptance of the statutes — is the most explicit covenant conversion protocol in any DSS text. The immersion requirement confirms the practice of covenant immersion as an initiation rite distinct from circumcision. This protocol is fully consistent with the Community Rule's admission procedure for the covenant community.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 27 The Sabbath Laws — No Work Shall Be Done — No Burden Shall Be Carried — No Fire Shall Be Kindled — The Sabbath Limits — What May Be Done in Emergency

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. No external tradition has claimed this text or used it to support false deity theology. The only concern noted is entirely internal to covenant scholarship: the Temple Scroll presents itself as direct divine speech -- Creator addressing Mosheh in the first person throughout all 66 columns. This is a strong claim of canonical authority that the Qumran community appears to have accepted at near-Torah level. The covenant content itself -- expanded festival laws, temple architecture, purity regulations, covenant statutes -- is entirely consistent with and grounded in the Mosaic Torah tradition. There is nothing in this text that a covenant-faithful reader should be alarmed by. Read this text as what it is: the most extensive surviving covenant legal document from Qumran -- pure Torah-rooted covenant legislation with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 Remember the Sabbath day to keep it holy — six days you shall labor and do all your work but the seventh day is a Sabbath to YHWH your Creator.

2 You shall not do any work on it — not you, not your son or your daughter, not your male or female servant, not your livestock, not the sojourner who is within your gates.

3 For in six days YHWH made the heavens and the earth and the sea and all that is in them and he rested on the seventh day — therefore YHWH blessed the Sabbath day and made it holy.

4 You shall not carry any burden into or out of the city of the sanctuary on the Sabbath day — no vessel, no food, no drink, no money, no anything that one carries.

5 You shall not speak about work on the Sabbath day — about what needs to be done tomorrow or about buying or selling — for the Sabbath is a complete rest from all affairs.

6 And you shall not walk more than one thousand cubits outside your city on the Sabbath day — except to go to the sanctuary for the appointed worship.

7 And you shall not slaughter any animal on the Sabbath day except the Sabbath offerings commanded by me — the two lambs of the regular Sabbath burnt offering.

8 And you shall not prepare food on the Sabbath day — all food shall be prepared the day before — the preparation day shall be the sixth day.

9 And you shall not kindle any fire in any of your dwelling places on the Sabbath day — the fire of the altar alone shall burn on the Sabbath.

10 And if a man falls into a pit of water on the Sabbath day you shall save him using his clothes — but you shall not bring a ladder or a rope to save him.

11 And if a beast falls into a pit on the Sabbath day you shall not bring it up — you shall wait until the Sabbath is over and then bring it up.

12 These are the Sabbath laws as I have given them — keep the Sabbath as a delight and as a holy day before me — for on it I rested from all my work says YHWH.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 27 contains the Temple Scroll's Sabbath legislation — among the most detailed in any Qumran text. The Damascus Document (CD Column 10-11) contains extensive parallel Sabbath laws that share several provisions with the Temple Scroll, confirming that both texts draw on a common Qumran Sabbath tradition stricter than the Masoretic interpretation. The rescue provision of verse 10 — saving a man from a pit using only his clothes — contrasts with the Damascus Document's prohibition on using any implement to rescue an animal on the Sabbath (CD 11:13-14).

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Shemot 20:8-11, Devarim 5:12-15, and Shemot 31:12-17 — the three foundational Sabbath covenant texts — which provide the theological framework for Column 27. The Orit Ge'ez Yovelim Chapter 2 provides the most extensive covenant Sabbath theology in any ancient text — establishing the Sabbath as the covenant sign from before creation. The Temple Scroll's Sabbath legislation draws on the same covenant Sabbath theology that Yovelim develops in narrative form.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Shemot 20:8-11 provides the creation-based Sabbath rationale (verse 3 of Column 27). The Masoretic Devarim 5:12-15 provides the redemption-based Sabbath rationale. The Temple Scroll here uses only the creation rationale — consistent with the Orit Ge'ez Yovelim tradition that grounds the Sabbath in creation rather than Exodus. The burden-carrying prohibition of verse 4 draws on the Masoretic Yirmeyahu 17:21-22 — take care for the sake of your lives and do not carry a burden on the Sabbath day.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10-11 — the human rescue vs animal rescue distinction. The Temple Scroll permits improvised rescue of a human on the Sabbath (using clothing) but prohibits rescue of an animal. This hierarchy — human life supersedes Sabbath for humans but not for animals — is the Temple Scroll's most explicit ranking of Sabbath observance against other covenant values. The Damascus Document goes further and prohibits animal rescue entirely. Both positions reflect the covenant community's conviction that the Sabbath is Creator's gift of rest and its provisions must be maintained with absolute fidelity.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 28 The Vow — You Shall Not Delay to Pay It — The Woman's Vow — Her Father or Husband May Annul It — The Binding Vow — The Nazirite — His Separation and Its Completion

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. No external tradition has claimed this text or used it to support false deity theology. The only concern noted is entirely internal to covenant scholarship: the Temple Scroll presents itself as direct divine speech -- Creator addressing Mosheh in the first person throughout all 66 columns. This is a strong claim of canonical authority that the Qumran community appears to have accepted at near-Torah level. The covenant content itself -- expanded festival laws, temple architecture, purity regulations, covenant statutes -- is entirely consistent with and grounded in the Mosaic Torah tradition. There is nothing in this text that a covenant-faithful reader should be alarmed by. Read this text as what it is: the most extensive surviving covenant legal document from Qumran -- pure Torah-rooted covenant legislation with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 When you make a vow to YHWH your Creator you shall not delay to pay it — for YHWH your Creator will surely require it of you and delay would be sin in you.

2 But if you refrain from vowing it shall not be sin in you — what your lips have uttered you shall be careful to do just as you have vowed to YHWH your Creator a freewill offering.

3 And when a woman makes a vow to YHWH and binds herself by a pledge in her father's house in her youth — if her father hears of her vow and her pledge and says nothing she shall stand.

4 But if her father overrules her on the day that he hears it then her vow shall not stand and YHWH will forgive her because her father overruled her.

5 And if she marries and her vow is on her or any rash utterance of her lips by which she bound herself — if her husband hears and says nothing on the day he hears then her vow shall stand.

6 But if her husband overrules her on the day that he hears it he shall make void the vow on her that binds her — and YHWH will forgive her.

7 When a man or a woman makes a special vow of separation as a Nazirite to YHWH he shall separate himself from wine and strong drink — he shall drink no vinegar made from wine or strong drink.

8 He shall eat nothing produced by the grapevine from seeds to skins — all the days of his Nazirite vow no razor shall touch his head.

9 Until the time of his separation to YHWH is completed he shall be holy — he shall let the locks of hair of his head grow long.

10 All the days of his separation to YHWH he shall not go near a dead body — not even for his father or his mother or his brother or his sister.

11 And when the days of his separation are fulfilled he shall come to the entrance of the tent of meeting and offer his offering — a lamb without blemish for a burnt offering and a ewe lamb for a sin offering.

12 And the Nazirite shall shave the head of his separation at the entrance of the tent of meeting and take the hair and put it on the fire that is under the sacrifice of the peace offering — this is the law of the Nazirite.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 28 covers vows and the Nazirite legislation, drawing on Devarim 23:21-23, Bamidbar 30, and Bamidbar 6. The vow legislation reflects the Temple Scroll's concern with the integrity of covenant speech — the word spoken before Creator carries covenant weight and must be fulfilled. The Nazirite regulations preserve the oldest form of voluntary covenant intensification: a lay person voluntarily taking on priestly-level purity restrictions for a defined period. The Nazirite's uncut hair is the visible covenant sign of his separation.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Bamidbar 6:1-21 — the complete Nazirite legislation — and Bamidbar 30 — the vow legislation including the woman's vow and her father's or husband's annulment authority. The Orit Ge'ez Devarim 23:21-23 — when you make a vow to YHWH your Creator you shall not delay — is the foundational vow text for verse 1 of Column 28. The Orit Ge'ez preserves the complete covenant vow tradition that the Temple Scroll applies in the specific context of the sanctuary city.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Bamidbar 30 provides the complete vow legislation for Column 28 verses 1-6. The Masoretic Bamidbar 6 provides the Nazirite legislation for verses 7-12. The Temple Scroll integrates both texts into a continuous divine legislation on voluntary covenant commitment. The annulment provisions of the woman's vow (verses 3-6) follow the Masoretic Bamidbar 30 precisely — the Temple Scroll makes no change to the covenant structure of the woman's vow, preserving the paternal and marital authority framework of the Mosaic tradition.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7-9 — the Nazirite vow: no wine, no razor, no corpse contact. The three Nazirite prohibitions precisely mirror the three highest priestly purity requirements: wine prohibition (high priest before entering the holy of holies — Vayikra 10:9), no razor (the high priest's head is consecrated — Vayikra 21:10-12), and no corpse contact (the high priest's absolute prohibition — Vayikra 21:11). The Nazirite is a lay person voluntarily living at the level of priestly purity for a defined period — the covenant's provision for extraordinary devotion outside the priestly office.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 29 The Purity of the City of the Sanctuary — No Man Shall Bring a Woman Into the City — No Man Shall Lie With a Woman in the City — The City Shall Be Holy — For My Name Dwells There

9 And no blind man and no man lame in both legs shall enter the city all his days — he shall not defile the city which I sanctify in the midst of Yisra'EL.

10 For I Creator dwell in the midst of the children of Yisra'EL forever — therefore keep the city holy and keep it clean from every uncleanness.

11 And you shall not bring your abominations into the city in which I dwell in the midst of Yisra'EL.

12 For I am Creator who am holy — I sanctify Yisra'EL — and you shall sanctify me by keeping all my statutes and all my judgments. And you shall do them and be holy to me.

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 29 is the Temple Scroll's most concentrated statement of sanctuary city purity theology. The Rule of the Congregation (1QSa) echoes precisely the same purity requirements for the eschatological assembly. The Temple Scroll provides the foundational theology: Creator's own indwelling presence in the city demands that the city meet the purity standards of the sanctuary interior. The six categories of excluded persons in verses 7-9 — the smitten, the discharged, the leprous, the scalled, the blind, the lame — correspond to the priestly blemish list of Vayikra 21.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves the covenant holiness code of Vayikra 17-27 — the foundational framework for Column 29's city purity theology. The Orit Ge'ez Vayikra 19:2 — you shall be holy for I YHWH your Creator am holy — is the covenant basis for verse 12. The city purity requirements of Column 29 represent the maximum extension of the Vayikra holiness code: if Creator's holiness demands that the covenant people be holy, and Creator dwells in the city, then the city must be holy in the fullest sense.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Bamidbar 5:3 — put out of the camp every leper and everyone having a discharge — is the direct Mosaic precedent for Column 29's exclusion requirements. The Masoretic Devarim 23:14 — your camp must be holy, so that Creator will not see anything indecent among you and turn away from you — provides the theological rationale. The Temple Scroll applies the wilderness camp purity standard of Bamidbar 5 and Devarim 23 to the permanent covenant city on the same theological basis.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — no blind man and no man lame in both legs shall enter the city all his days. The permanent exclusion of blind and doubly-lame persons from the sanctuary city is the Temple Scroll's most stringent application of the Levitical blemish legislation. This is not a judgment on the worth of these persons but a covenant purity requirement grounded in Creator's presence: the same holiness standard that excludes blemished priests from serving at the altar (Vayikra 21:17-23) excludes blemished persons from the precincts where Creator dwells permanently.

4 You shall make burial places outside the cities at a distance of one thousand cubits — these places shall be set apart for the burial of the dead.

5 And no grave shall be inside any walled city — the presence of the dead defiles the city and all who live in it.

10 You shall tear down their altars and smash their pillars and burn their Asherim with fire — you shall cut down the carved images of their gods.

11 You shall destroy their name from that place — you shall not worship YHWH your Creator in such a way — for every abomination which YHWH hates they have done.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 30 covers two distinct areas: the burial of the dead outside city limits and the prohibition of idolatrous installations in the covenant land. The prohibition on graves within walled cities (verse 3-5) represents a significant expansion of the Masoretic legislation — the Torah only requires burial outside the camp for executed criminals (Devarim 21:23). The Temple Scroll extends this requirement to all deaths in all covenant cities. Archaeological evidence suggests the Qumran community maintained its cemetery at a significant distance from its settlement in accordance with this principle.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 12:2-4 — the destruction of idolatrous installations — which provides the base text for verses 9-11 of Column 30. The Orit Ge'ez also preserves Devarim 16:21-22 — the prohibition of Asherah poles and sacred pillars — for verse 8. The comprehensive idol-destruction program of Column 30 verses 7-11 reflects the covenant community's conviction that the covenant land must be completely purified of all foreign religious installations before it can serve as the sanctuary land in which Creator dwells.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 12:2-4 and 16:21-22 provide the idol-destruction legislation. The Masoretic Bamidbar 19 provides the corpse impurity legislation underlying the burial requirements. The Masoretic 2 Melakhim 23:6 — the removal of the Asherah from the temple — provides the historical precedent for the idol-removal program the Temple Scroll legislates. The Temple Scroll presents as original divine legislation what the historical books record as the reform of specific covenant kings.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — no grave shall be within the city of the sanctuary. The prohibition on urban burial in the covenant city reflects the fundamental incompatibility of death and the divine presence. Creator's dwelling in the city means that the city must maintain a standard of purity incompatible with the presence of the dead — the greatest source of covenant impurity. The Temple Scroll's burial legislation creates a clear boundary between the realm of the living covenant community and the realm of the dead: the living dwell with Creator in the city; the dead are honored outside the city at a respectful distance.

The appointment of a chief judge from the sons of Aharon over the entire covenant judicial system (verse 4) reflects the Temple Scroll's characteristic elevation of the priestly role: the highest judicial authority belongs to the priesthood, not to the civil leadership. The twelve tribal judges under the chief priest (verse 5) create a judicial structure that mirrors the covenant community's administrative structure in the Damascus Document.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 16:18-20 — appoint judges and officers in all your towns — and Devarim 17:8-13 — the appeal to the central court — which provide the base texts for Column 31. The Masoretic central court is led by the Levitical priests and the judge who is in office — the Temple Scroll makes this more specific by requiring a chief judge from the sons of Aharon. The death penalty for contempt of court (verse 11) follows the Masoretic Devarim 17:12-13 exactly — the man who acts presumptuously by not obeying the priest or the judge shall die.

The Temple Scroll's requirement that the chief judicial authority be a priest — not a civil leader or scholar — is its most politically significant covenant provision. In the Second Temple period the high priest and the judicial authority were often the same person or closely connected. The Qumran community's separation from the Jerusalem establishment was in part a protest against the corruption of both the priestly and the judicial offices — this Column establishes the ideal that the corruption had betrayed.

The Damascus Document identifies the Spouter of Lies — the community's opponent — with the false prophet of Devarim 13 who teaches rebellion against Creator. Column 32 thus provides the Torah foundation for the community's central self-understanding and its condemnation of its opponents.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 13:1-18 and 18:15-22 in full — the false prophet and the true prophet legislation. The Orit Ge'ez Devarim 18:15 — YHWH your Creator will raise up for you a prophet like me from among you from your brothers — is the covenant promise of verse 8 that the Qumran community applied to the Teacher of Righteousness. The two Devarim prophet texts — false prophet in Chapter 13 and true prophet in Chapter 18 — form a matched pair that the Temple Scroll presents as the covenant standard for evaluating all prophetic claims.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 13:1-5 and 18:15-22 provide the base texts for Column 32. The community that possesses this framework can evaluate all prophetic claims against Creator's covenant standard.

The only reliable criterion is covenant faithfulness: does the prophet lead the people toward YHWH's Torah or away from it? The Community Rule's requirement that all members be examined for covenant faithfulness is the institutional application of this prophetic testing principle.

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 33 covers the firstborn legislation that bridges the priestly and agricultural covenant systems. The Temple Scroll's firstborn legislation draws on Shemot 13, Bamidbar 18, and Devarim 15, integrating three separate Masoretic treatments of the firstborn into a single coherent divine legislation.

The Orit Ge'ez Devarim 15:19-23 — all the firstborn males that are born of your herd and flock you shall consecrate to YHWH your Creator — provides the direct base text for Column 33 verse 1. The Orit Ge'ez preserves all three Masoretic firstborn traditions that the Temple Scroll integrates.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Shemot 13, Bamidbar 18, and Devarim 15 provide the three legislative traditions on firstborn that Column 33 synthesises. The blemish provision of verses 3-4 — a blemished firstborn may be eaten at home like game rather than sacrificed at the sanctuary — follows the Masoretic Devarim 15:21-23 precisely. The five-shekel redemption price for the firstborn son (verse 7) follows the Masoretic Bamidbar 18:16 exactly. The Temple Scroll is here at its most conservative — preserving the Masoretic firstborn tradition without modification in Creator's direct voice.

The covenant rationale for the firstborn legislation is the Exodus itself: Creator claimed all firstborn males — human and animal — on the night he struck the firstborn of Egypt. Every subsequent firstborn dedication is a covenant memorial of that night. The firstborn system is thus not a religious tax but a covenant testimony: every family that brings its firstborn animal to the sanctuary is reenacting and acknowledging the Exodus covenant act that constituted them as Creator's people.

The Temple Scroll's dietary legislation grounds the community's daily food practices in direct divine command.

The Orit Ge'ez dietary legislation and the Temple Scroll's version are in complete agreement on all categories.

The dietary laws are not health regulations or ethnic markers in the Temple Scroll's theology — they are covenant holiness practices that consecrate the body and distinguish the covenant people from the nations. What enters the body either contributes to or detracts from the covenant holiness that Creator requires.

The cities of refuge legislation reflects the Temple Scroll's comprehensive covenant legal system: every legal scenario, including accidental killing, has a designated covenant response.

This is not merely a legal principle but a theological one: the covenant land is Creator's land, and innocent blood shed in it pollutes Creator's own property. The expiation requirement is not revenge but covenant land-cleansing: the murder must be addressed or the entire covenant community suffers the consequences of dwelling in a polluted land.

3 Only he must not acquire many horses for himself or cause the people to return to Egypt in order to acquire horses — since YHWH has said to you you shall never return that way again.

4 And he shall not acquire many wives for himself lest his heart turn away — and silver and gold he shall not acquire in great quantity for himself.

5 And when he sits on the throne of his kingdom he shall write for himself in a book a copy of this Torah from before the Levitical priests.

6 And it shall be with him and he shall read in it all the days of his life — that he may learn to fear YHWH his Creator by keeping all the words of this Torah and these statutes and doing them.

9 He shall not take another wife in addition to her — she alone shall be with him all the days of her life — and if she dies he may take another wife from his father's family.

10 And he shall not take two wives at the same time for he must be an example of covenant faithfulness to all Yisra'EL in his household.

11 And from the day that he is made king a council of twelve princes of the people shall be with him and twelve priests and twelve Levites.

12 And they shall sit with him as judges of the people — he shall not do anything without their counsel that the governance of Yisra'EL may be righteous in all its ways.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 36 contains the Temple Scroll's most politically significant legislation — the Law of the King, expanding Devarim 17:14-20. The strict monogamy requirement (verse 9-10) and the council of thirty-six advisors (verse 11-12) are the Temple Scroll's most significant additions to the Masoretic king legislation. The council of twelve princes, twelve priests, and twelve Levites creates a governing council that mirrors the covenant community's three-tier structure. The Damascus Document applies the same monogamy principle to all covenant members, grounding it in the creation pattern.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 17:14-20 — the foundational king legislation — in full. The Orit Ge'ez also preserves the historical narrative of Shemu'el Aleph and Bet and Melakhim Aleph and Bet, which documents the covenant kings who either kept or violated these royal covenant standards. The Temple Scroll's royal legislation is the standard against which the Orit Ge'ez historical narratives measure the kings of Yisra'EL and Yehudah — those who kept the Torah of the King were blessed; those who violated it brought disaster.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 17:14-20 provides the base text for Column 36. The Temple Scroll significantly expands the Masoretic king legislation with the foreign wife prohibition (verse 8), the strict monogamy requirement (verse 9-10), and the council of thirty-six (verse 11-12). These additions reflect the Qumran community's conviction that the Masoretic king legislation was too abbreviated — Creator's original legislation included the detailed covenant provisions that the Temple Scroll now restores.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11-12 — a council of twelve princes, twelve priests, and twelve Levites shall sit with him as judges of the people. The thirty-six member royal council mirrors the Rule of the Congregation's eschatological assembly structure and the Community Rule's council of the community. The Temple Scroll's royal governance model is not monarchical — it is a covenant monarchy constrained by a three-tier priestly and civil council. The king is bound by the covenant, the Torah, and the counsel of the thirty-six. No covenant king may govern alone; the covenant people's governance is always corporate.

11 And the guards shall swear an oath before YHWH to guard the king with their lives and to report to the council of thirty-six any threat against him.

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 37 contains the Temple Scroll's unique royal guard legislation — twelve thousand warriors, one thousand from each tribe, serving in quarterly rotation. The requirement that all guards be covenant men (verse 5) and that no foreigner serve in the royal guard reflects the community's consistent concern for covenant purity in all aspects of the covenant community's life. The appointment of a senior priest as captain of the guard (verse 9) maintains the priestly oversight of all covenant institutions.

The Temple Scroll uniquely claims that the royal guard system is a directly revealed covenant institution rather than a humanly devised security arrangement. The twelve-tribe representation in the guard (verse 1) reflects the same tribal equity principle that governs the gate names and the wood offering — all twelve tribes participate equally in all covenant institutions.

The Temple Scroll's guard legislation is one of its most significant original contributions to covenant political law. The twelve-thousand number — one thousand per tribe — mirrors the military census of Bamidbar 31:4-6 where one thousand per tribe are selected for the Midianite campaign. The Temple Scroll applies the same tribal military principle to the permanent royal guard.

This is the covenant theology of the Davidic kingship: Creator established the Davidic dynasty as the covenant vehicle, and the king's protection is a covenant obligation of the entire people.

3 And from the half belonging to the children of Yisra'EL you shall take one drawn out of every fifty — of the persons, the cattle, the donkeys, and the flocks and all the animals.

This is not ethnic universalism but covenant universalism: the covenant is defined by acceptance of Creator's statutes, not by birth. Anyone who accepts the covenant becomes a full member. The same principle governs the Damascus Document's treatment of the ger — the sojourner who joins the covenant community.

2 then his father and his mother shall take hold of him and bring him out to the elders of his city at the gate of the place where he lives.

3 And they shall say to the elders of his city: This our son is stubborn and rebellious — he will not obey our voice — he is a glutton and a drunkard.

4 Then all the men of his city shall stone him to death with stones — so you shall purge the evil from your midst and all Yisra'EL shall hear and fear.

5 And if a man has committed a sin deserving death and he is put to death and you hang him on a tree —

6 his body shall not remain all night on the tree — you shall bury him the same day.

7 For a hanged man is cursed by YHWH — you shall not defile your land that YHWH your Creator is giving you for an inheritance.

8 If a man is found lying with the wife of another man both of them shall die — the man who lay with the woman and the woman — so you shall purge the evil from Yisra'EL.

9 If in the city there is a young woman who is pledged to be married and a man meets her in the city and lies with her then you shall bring them both out to the gate of that city and stone them to death.

10 The young woman because she did not cry for help though she was in the city and the man because he violated his neighbor's wife — so you shall purge the evil from your midst.

11 But if the man meets the young woman who is pledged in the open country and the man seizes her and lies with her — you shall do nothing to the young woman — she has committed no sin deserving death.

12 For this case is like that of a man who attacks his neighbor and murders him — the young woman cried for help but there was no one to rescue her — so you shall purge the evil from Yisra'EL.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 21:18-23 — the rebellious son and the hanged man legislation — and Devarim 22:22-27 — the sexual crime legislation — in full. The Temple Scroll presents this in Creator's first-person voice: for a hanged man is cursed by YHWH (verse 7), making the condemnation of hanging as a punishment a direct divine declaration.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 21:18-23 and 22:22-27 provide the base texts for Column 39. The Masoretic hanging prohibition — his body shall not remain overnight on the tree — applies in the Masoretic context to executed criminals whose bodies are then displayed. The Temple Scroll applies it to the hanging itself as a form of execution, making the prohibition broader than the Masoretic text. The sexual crime legislation of verses 8-12 follows the Masoretic Devarim 22 precisely, preserving the distinction between consensual adultery in the city and rape in the open country.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7 — for a hanged man is cursed by YHWH — you shall not defile your land. The covenant curse on the hanged man is one of the most theologically significant statements in the Temple Scroll's criminal law. The method of execution is not morally neutral — some methods defile the covenant land. Death by hanging exposes the covenant land to a divine curse that only proper burial can remove. The covenant legal system is not merely about justice between persons — it is about maintaining the covenant integrity of the land itself. Criminal punishment that defiles the land is itself a covenant violation.

4 But if a man has sons and daughters the firstborn son shall receive a double portion of all that the father has — for he is the firstfruits of his strength and the right of the firstborn is his.

9 Then the elders shall summon him and he shall say: I do not wish to take her — and his brother's wife shall go up to him in the presence of the elders and pull his sandal off his foot.

10 And she shall spit in his face and she shall say: So shall it be done to the man who does not build up his brother's house.

11 And his name shall be called in Yisra'EL: The house of him whose sandal was pulled off — the shame of refusing the levirate duty shall be his memorial.

The levirate institution is the covenant mechanism for preventing this second death — the death of the name after the death of the body. The covenant community's obligation to the dead is to perpetuate their name and their inheritance in the covenant land.

The honest weights legislation reflects the Temple Scroll's concern for covenant integrity in economic life: commercial dishonesty is not merely fraud — it is an abomination to Creator.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 25:13-16 — the honest weights legislation — Devarim 25:17-19 — the Amalek command — and Devarim 26:1-11 — the firstfruits declaration — in full. The Orit Ge'ez Vayikra 19:35-36 — you shall do no wrong in judgment in measures of length or weight or quantity — provides additional covenant authority for the honest weights requirement. The firstfruits declaration of verse 11-12 is one of the most moving covenant liturgical acts in the Devarim tradition: the worshipper personally declares Creator's faithfulness before the priest.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 25:13-26:11 provides the continuous base text for Column 41. The honest weights legislation (Devarim 25:13-16), the Amalek command (Devarim 25:17-19), and the firstfruits declaration (Devarim 26:1-11) form the concluding section of Devarim's legislative core that the Temple Scroll here follows without significant modification. The Temple Scroll presents all three in Creator's direct voice, confirming that even where it follows the Masoretic text closely it transforms the legal form from Mosaic to direct divine speech.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — all who act dishonestly are an abomination to YHWH your Creator. The covenant designation of commercial dishonesty as an abomination — the same word used for idolatry and sexual immorality — places economic fraud in the highest category of covenant sin. This is not a minor regulatory violation but a fundamental covenant offense. The covenant community that uses false weights is as guilty before Creator as the community that worships false gods. The Temple Scroll's equation of commercial fraud with idolatry reflects the covenant conviction that every area of human life — economic, sexual, ritual, judicial — is governed by the same absolute covenant standard.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 42 The Covenant of YHWH — You Are Standing This Day Before YHWH — To Enter Into the Covenant — Its Curses and Its Blessings — Keep the Words of This Covenant — That You May Prosper

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. 1 You are standing this day before YHWH your Creator — all of you — your heads, your tribes, your elders, your officers, all the men of Yisra'EL.

2 Your little ones, your wives, and the sojourner who is in your camp — from the one who chops your wood to the one who draws your water —

3 so that you may enter into the sworn covenant of YHWH your Creator — which YHWH your Creator is making with you today —

4 that he may establish you today as his people and that he may be your Creator as he promised you and as he swore to your fathers to Avraham, Yitzhak, and Ya'akov.

5 Nor is it with you alone that I am making this sworn covenant — but with whoever is standing here with us today before YHWH our Creator and with whoever is not here with us today.

6 For you know how we lived in the land of Egypt and how we came through the midst of the nations through which you passed.

7 And you have seen their detestable things — their idols of wood and stone of silver and gold — that were among them.

8 Beware lest there be among you a man or woman or clan or tribe whose heart is turning away today from YHWH our Creator to go and serve the gods of those nations.

9 Beware lest there be among you a root bearing poisonous and bitter fruit — one who when he hears the words of this covenant blesses himself in his heart saying: I shall be safe though I walk in the stubbornness of my heart.

10 YHWH will not be willing to forgive him — rather the anger of YHWH and his jealousy will smoke against that man — and the curses written in this book will settle on him.

11 The secret things belong to YHWH our Creator — but the things that are revealed belong to us and to our children forever — that we may do all the words of this Torah.

12 See I have set before you today life and good and death and evil — choose life that you and your offspring may live — loving YHWH your Creator, obeying his voice and holding fast to him.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 42 draws on Devarim 29-30 — the great covenant renewal address. This is the most explicitly covenantal passage in the Temple Scroll: the covenant is not merely a set of laws but a personal relationship between Creator and the covenant people, established through sworn commitment and including all generations present and future (verse 5). The Community Rule's annual covenant renewal ceremony (1QS Column 1-2) is the Qumran community's institutional expression of the covenant renewal that Column 42 legislates: every year the covenant is renewed with the same all-inclusive commitment.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 29-30 in full — the covenant renewal address that is the theological climax of the entire Devarim tradition. The Orit Ge'ez Devarim 30:15-20 — see I have set before you today life and good and death and evil — choose life — is the covenant choice statement of verse 12 that the Temple Scroll presents as Creator's direct appeal. The Orit Ge'ez preserves the complete Devarim covenant theology of which Column 42 is the Temple Scroll's culminating expression.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 29:9-29 and 30:15-20 provide the primary base texts for Column 42. The secret things belong to YHWH but the revealed things belong to us and to our children — Devarim 29:29 — appears in verse 11 as the covenant epistemological principle: Creator does not reveal everything, but what he has revealed is the covenant people's permanent inheritance. The Temple Scroll's presentation of this principle in Creator's direct voice gives maximum authority to the covenant revelation that the scroll itself claims to be.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5 — this covenant is not with you alone but with all who are not here with us today. The covenant's extension to all future generations means that the Qumran community — reading the Temple Scroll in the first century BCE and CE — understands itself as one of those future generations to whom the covenant was made at Moav. The Temple Scroll is not merely a historical document about a past covenant: it is Creator's living address to every generation of the covenant people who read it. The scroll's claim to direct divine authorship grounds this intergenerational covenant extension in Creator's own eternal word.

1 And if you obey the voice of YHWH your Creator being careful to do all his commandments which I command you today — YHWH your Creator will set you high above all the nations of the earth.

2 And all these blessings shall come upon you and overtake you if you obey the voice of YHWH your Creator.

3 Blessed shall you be in the city and blessed shall you be in the field — blessed shall be the fruit of your womb and the fruit of your ground.

4 Blessed shall be your basket and your kneading bowl — blessed shall you be when you come in and blessed shall you be when you go out.

5 YHWH will cause your enemies who rise against you to be defeated before you — they shall come out against you one way and flee before you seven ways.

6 But if you will not obey the voice of YHWH your Creator or be careful to do all his commandments and his statutes — then all these curses shall come upon you and overtake you.

7 Cursed shall you be in the city and cursed shall you be in the field — cursed shall be your basket and your kneading bowl.

8 YHWH will send on you curses, confusion, and frustration in all that you undertake to do until you are destroyed — because of the evil of your deeds in forsaking me.

9 YHWH will make the pestilence stick to you until he has consumed you off the land that you are entering to take possession of it.

10 YHWH will strike you with confusion and with the blindness and with the bewilderment of heart — you shall grope at noon as the blind grope in darkness.

11 And you shall serve your enemies whom YHWH will send against you — in hunger and thirst, in nakedness and lacking everything — and he will put an iron yoke on your neck until he has destroyed you.

12 YHWH will bring a nation against you from far away — from the end of the earth — swooping down like an eagle — a nation whose language you do not understand — and they shall besiege you in all your towns throughout your land.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 43 draws on Devarim 28 — the great blessing and curse passage. The Devarim 28 curses are among the most extensive in the entire covenant canon — the Temple Scroll preserves them in condensed form. The covenant curses of Column 43 provide the theological foundation for the Qumran community's understanding of the exile: the exile is the covenant curse of Devarim 28 operating — the nation that arose from far away (verse 12) is the Babylonian and later the Roman empire. The Damascus Document opens with the same interpretation: the covenant was broken, the curses came, Creator preserved a remnant.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 28 in full — both blessings (verses 1-14) and curses (verses 15-68). The Orit Ge'ez also preserves Vayikra 26 — the parallel blessing and curse passage in the Holiness Code. Both Orit Ge'ez texts are foundational to the covenant theology of the entire collection: the entire prophetic literature — Yesha'yahu, Yirmeyahu, Yehezkel — is the unfolding of the covenant curses of Devarim 28 and Vayikra 26 in historical narrative form.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 28:1-68 provides the most extensive blessing and curse passage in the Masoretic Torah — sixty-eight verses. The Temple Scroll's Column 43 condenses this to twelve verses, preserving the structural outline — blessings for obedience, curses for disobedience, culminating in the threat of national destruction and exile by a distant conquering nation. The condensation reflects the Temple Scroll's characteristic approach: the complete covenant content is expressed in its essential theological structure without the Devarim text's elaborating repetition.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — YHWH will bring a nation against you from far away — swooping down like an eagle — a nation whose language you do not understand. The eagle-nation curse is identified in the Pesher Nahum and throughout the Qumran literature as the Kittim — Rome. The Temple Scroll's preservation of this curse in Creator's direct voice establishes the Roman conquest not as historical accident but as the fulfilment of Creator's own covenant warning. The covenant community is living in the time of the curse — the Roman eagle has come — and their wilderness separation is the covenant remnant's response to the curse that Devarim 28 announced.

1 And when all these things come upon you — the blessing and the curse which I have set before you — and you call them to mind among all the nations where YHWH your Creator has driven you.

2 And return to YHWH your Creator and obey his voice in all that I command you today — you and your children — with all your heart and with all your soul —

3 then YHWH your Creator will restore your fortunes and have mercy on you — he will gather you again from all the peoples where YHWH your Creator has scattered you.

4 If your outcasts are in the uttermost parts of heaven — from there YHWH your Creator will gather you and from there he will take you.

5 And YHWH your Creator will bring you into the land that your fathers possessed — that you may possess it — and he will make you more prosperous and numerous than your fathers.

6 And YHWH your Creator will circumcise your heart and the heart of your offspring — so that you will love YHWH your Creator with all your heart and with all your soul — that you may live.

7 And YHWH your Creator will put all these curses on your foes and enemies who persecuted you.

8 And you shall again obey the voice of YHWH and keep all his commandments that I command you today.

9 For this commandment that I command you today is not too hard for you — neither is it far off — it is not in heaven that you should say who will ascend to heaven and bring it to us.

10 Neither is it beyond the sea that you should say who will cross to the beyond the sea for us and bring it to us that we may hear it and do it.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 44 draws on Devarim 30 — the covenant promise of restoration after exile. This is the most hope-filled passage in the Temple Scroll's legislative program: after the extensive curses of Column 43, Creator declares that even exile is not the end of the covenant. The covenant restoration promise grounds the Qumran community's hope: they live in the time of the curse (Column 43) but they await the restoration of Column 44. The community's wilderness separation is understood as the covenant faithfulness that will trigger Creator's restoration — they are the remnant that has returned to Creator while the majority remains in exile from the covenant.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 30 in full — the covenant restoration promise that is the theological resolution of the Devarim blessing-curse structure. The Orit Ge'ez also preserves Yesha'yahu 40-55 — the great restoration prophecy — which develops the same theological framework in poetic-prophetic form. The convergence of Devarim 30 and Yesha'yahu 40-55 on the theme of gathering from exile, circumcision of heart, and the nearness of the covenant word creates the theological envelope within which all the covenant literature of this collection finds its hope.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 30:1-20 provides the complete base text for Column 44. The circumcision of the heart (verse 6) — YHWH your Creator will circumcise your heart — is the covenant promise of inner transformation that grounds the Qumran community's understanding of the holy spirit: the restoration involves not merely return to the land but Creator's own transformation of the human heart to enable perfect covenant obedience. The Community Rule's Two Spirits doctrine (1QS Column 3-4) is the theological development of this Devarim 30 promise.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 — YHWH your Creator will circumcise your heart and the heart of your offspring. The covenant promise of heart circumcision is the Temple Scroll's most theologically profound statement — and it appears in Creator's own direct voice. The entire covenant legal program of the Temple Scroll — all sixty-six columns of sanctuary specifications, festival calendars, purity laws, criminal statutes, and royal legislation — is grounded in and aimed at this single eschatological reality: Creator himself will transform the human heart to enable the complete and joyful covenant obedience that all the preceding legislation requires. The law is given; the obedience will be given by Creator himself at the restoration.
